**Science fiction** (often shortened to **sci-fi** or abbreviated **SF**) is the [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre") of [speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction") that imagines advanced and futuristic scientific or technological [progress](/wiki/Progress "Progress"). The elements common to science fiction have increased over time: from [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration"), [extraterrestrial life](/wiki/Extraterrestrials_in_fiction "Extraterrestrials in fiction"), [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel"), and [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics"); to [parallel universes](/wiki/Parallel_universes_in_fiction "Parallel universes in fiction"), [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian_fiction "Dystopian fiction") societies, and [biological](/wiki/Biological "Biological") manipulations; and, most lately, to [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [transhumanism](/wiki/Transhumanism "Transhumanism"), [posthumanism](/wiki/Posthumanism "Posthumanism"), and [environmental challenges](/wiki/Climate_fiction "Climate fiction"). Science fiction often explores human responses to the consequences of these types of projected or imagined scientific advances.

The precise [definition of science fiction](/wiki/Definitions_of_science_fiction "Definitions of science fiction") has long been disputed among authors, critics, scholars, and readers. It contains many [subgenres](#Subgenres), including [*hard* science fiction](/wiki/Hard_science_fiction "Hard science fiction"), which emphasizes scientific accuracy, and [*soft* science fiction](/wiki/Soft_science_fiction "Soft science fiction"), which focuses on social sciences. Other notable subgenres are: [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera"), which emphasizes pure adventure in a universe in which space travel is common; [cyberpunk](/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk"), which explores the interface between technology and society; and [climate fiction](/wiki/Climate_fiction "Climate fiction"), which addresses environmental issues.

Precedents for science fiction are claimed to exist as far back as antiquity. Some books written in the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and the [Enlightenment Age](/wiki/Enlightenment_Age "Enlightenment Age") were considered early [science-fantasy](/wiki/Science-fantasy "Science-fantasy") stories. The modern genre arose primarily in the 19th and early 20th centuries, when popular writers began looking to technological progress for inspiration and speculation. [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley")'s *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")*, written in 1818, is often credited as the first true science fiction novel. [Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne") and [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") are pivotal figures in the genre's development. In the 20th century, the genre grew during the [Golden Age of Science Fiction](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Science_Fiction "Golden Age of Science Fiction"), and it expanded with the introduction of space operas, dystopian literature, and [pulp magazines](/wiki/Pulp_magazines "Pulp magazines").

Science fiction has come to influence not only literature, but also film, television, and culture at large. Science fiction can criticize present-day society and explore alternatives, as well as provide entertainment and inspire a *[sense of wonder](/wiki/Sense_of_wonder "Sense of wonder")*.

## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definitions")]

Main article: [Definitions of science fiction](/wiki/Definitions_of_science_fiction "Definitions of science fiction")

*[The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction](/wiki/The_Encyclopedia_of_Science_Fiction "The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction")*, edited by [John Clute](/wiki/John_Clute "John Clute") and [Peter Nicholls](/wiki/Peter_Nicholls_(writer) "Peter Nicholls (writer)") in 1993, contains an extensive discussion of the problem of defining the genre.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

American writer and professor of biochemistry [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") wrote, "Science fiction can be defined as that branch of literature which deals with the reaction of human beings to changes in science and technology."[[3]](#cite_note-IANH-3)

Science fiction writer [Robert A. Heinlein](/wiki/Robert_A._Heinlein "Robert A. Heinlein") wrote, "A handy short definition of almost all science fiction might read: realistic speculation about possible future events, based solidly on adequate [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge") of the real world, past and present, and on a thorough understanding of the nature and significance of the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method")."[[4]](#cite_note-heinlein_def-4)

American science fiction author and editor [Lester del Rey](/wiki/Lester_del_Rey "Lester del Rey") wrote, "Even the devoted aficionado or fan—has a hard time trying to explain what science fiction is," and no "full satisfactory definition" exists because "there are no easily delineated limits to science fiction."[[5]](#cite_note-The_World_of_Science_Fiction_1926–1976-5)

Another definition is provided in *The Literature Book* by the publisher [DK](/wiki/DK_(publisher) "DK (publisher)"): "scenarios that are at the time of writing technologically impossible, extrapolating from present-day science...[,]...or that deal with some form of speculative science-based conceit, such as a society (on Earth or another planet) that has developed in wholly different ways from our own."[[6]](#cite_note-6)

There is a tendency among science fiction enthusiasts to be their own arbiters in deciding what constitutes science fiction.[[7]](#cite_note-7) David Seed says that it may be more useful to talk about science fiction as the intersection of other more concrete subgenres.[[8]](#cite_note-8) American science fiction author, editor, and critic [Damon Knight](/wiki/Damon_Knight "Damon Knight") summed up the difficulty, saying "Science fiction is what we point to when we say it."[[9]](#cite_note-In_Search_of_Wonder:_Essays_on_Modern_Science_Fiction-9)

### Alternative terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alternative terms")]

Further information: [Skiffy](/wiki/Skiffy "Skiffy")

American magazine editor, science fiction writer, and literary agent [Forrest J Ackerman](/wiki/Forrest_J_Ackerman "Forrest J Ackerman") has been credited with first using the term *sci-fi* (reminiscent of the then-trendy term *[hi-fi](/wiki/Hi-fi "Hi-fi")*) in about 1954.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The first known use in print was a description of *[Donovan's Brain](/wiki/Donovan%27s_Brain_(film) "Donovan's Brain (film)")* by movie critic Jesse Zunser in January 1954.[[11]](#cite_note-11) As science fiction entered [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"), writers and fans in the field came to associate the term with low-quality [*pulp*](/wiki/Pulp_magazine "Pulp magazine") science fiction and with low-budget, low-tech [*B m*ovies](/wiki/B_movie "B movie").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) By the 1970s, [critics](/wiki/Critics "Critics") in the field, such as Damon Knight and [Terry Carr](/wiki/Terry_Carr "Terry Carr"), were using *sci fi* to [distinguish](/wiki/Distinguish "Distinguish") hack-work from serious science fiction.[[15]](#cite_note-wood_skiffy-15)

Australian literary scholar and critic [Peter Nicholls](/wiki/Peter_Nicholls_(writer) "Peter Nicholls (writer)") wrote ca. 1993 that *SF* (or *sf*) is "the preferred abbreviation within the community of sf writers and readers."[[16]](#cite_note-nicholls_sf-16)

Robert Heinlein found the term *science fiction* insufficient to describe certain types of works in this genre, and he suggested that the term *[speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction")* be used instead for works that are more "serious" or "thoughtful".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main articles: [History of science fiction](/wiki/History_of_science_fiction "History of science fiction") and [Timeline of science fiction](/wiki/Timeline_of_science_fiction "Timeline of science fiction")

### 18th century and earlier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 18th century and earlier")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Bacon_1628_New_Atlantis_title_page_wpreview.png/250px-Bacon_1628_New_Atlantis_title_page_wpreview.png)](/wiki/File:Bacon_1628_New_Atlantis_title_page_wpreview.png)

*[New Atlantis](/wiki/New_Atlantis "New Atlantis")* (1626) by [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon")

Some scholars assert that science fiction had its beginnings in [ancient times](/wiki/Ancient_times "Ancient times"), when the distinction between [myth](/wiki/Myth "Myth") and [fact](/wiki/Fact "Fact") was blurred.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Written in the 2nd century CE by the [satirist](/wiki/Satirist "Satirist") [Lucian](/wiki/Lucian "Lucian"), the novel *[A True Story](/wiki/A_True_Story "A True Story")* contains many themes and tropes that are characteristic of modern science fiction, including travel to other worlds, [extraterrestrial lifeforms](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life "Extraterrestrial life"), interplanetary warfare, and [artificial life](/wiki/Artificial_life_form "Artificial life form"). Some consider it to be the first science fiction novel.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Some stories from the folktale collection *[The Arabian Nights](/wiki/One_Thousand_and_One_Nights "One Thousand and One Nights")*,[[20]](#cite_note-The_Arabian_Nights:_A_Companion-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Richardson-21) along with the 10th-century fiction *[The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter](/wiki/The_Tale_of_the_Bamboo_Cutter "The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter")*[[21]](#cite_note-Richardson-21) and [Ibn al-Nafis](/wiki/Ibn_al-Nafis "Ibn al-Nafis")'s 13th-century novel *[Theologus Autodidactus](/wiki/Theologus_Autodidactus "Theologus Autodidactus")*,[[22]](#cite_note-Roubi-22) are also argued to contain elements of science fiction.

Several books written during the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and later the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") are considered true works of [science-fantasy](/wiki/Science-fantasy "Science-fantasy"). [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon")'s *[New Atlantis](/wiki/New_Atlantis "New Atlantis")* (1627),[[23]](#cite_note-The_Harmony_of_the_Worlds-23) [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler")'s *[Somnium](/wiki/Somnium_(novel) "Somnium (novel)")* (1634), [Athanasius Kircher](/wiki/Athanasius_Kircher "Athanasius Kircher")'s *Itinerarium extaticum* (1656),[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Cyrano de Bergerac](/wiki/Cyrano_de_Bergerac "Cyrano de Bergerac")'s *[Comical History of the States and Empires of the Moon](/wiki/Comical_History_of_the_States_and_Empires_of_the_Moon "Comical History of the States and Empires of the Moon")* (1657) and *[The States and Empires of the Sun](/wiki/The_States_and_Empires_of_the_Sun "The States and Empires of the Sun")* (1662), [Margaret Cavendish](/wiki/Margaret_Cavendish,_Duchess_of_Newcastle-upon-Tyne "Margaret Cavendish, Duchess of Newcastle-upon-Tyne")'s "[The Blazing World](/wiki/The_Blazing_World "The Blazing World")" (1666),[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Jonathan Swift](/wiki/Jonathan_Swift "Jonathan Swift")'s *[Gulliver's Travels](/wiki/Gulliver%27s_Travels "Gulliver's Travels")* (1726), [Ludvig Holberg](/wiki/Ludvig_Holberg "Ludvig Holberg")'s *[Nicolai Klimii Iter Subterraneum](/wiki/Niels_Klim%27s_Underground_Travels "Niels Klim's Underground Travels")* (1741) and [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire")'s *[Micromégas](/wiki/Microm%C3%A9gas "Micromégas")* (1752).[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") and [Carl Sagan](/wiki/Carl_Sagan "Carl Sagan") considered Johannes Kepler's 1634 novel *[Somnium](/wiki/Somnium_(novel) "Somnium (novel)")* to be the first science fiction story; it depicts a journey to the Moon and how the Earth's motion is seen from there.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Kepler has been called the "father of science fiction".[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Following the 17th-century development of the novel as a [literary form](/wiki/Literary_genre "Literary genre"), [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley")'s *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* (1818) and *[The Last Man](/wiki/The_Last_Man_(Mary_Shelley_novel) "The Last Man (Mary Shelley novel)")* (1826) helped to define the form of the science fiction novel. [Brian Aldiss](/wiki/Brian_Aldiss "Brian Aldiss") has argued that *Frankenstein* was the first work of science fiction.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") wrote several stories considered to be science fiction, including "[The Unparalleled Adventure of One Hans Pfaall](/wiki/The_Unparalleled_Adventure_of_One_Hans_Pfaall "The Unparalleled Adventure of One Hans Pfaall")" (1835) about a trip to the Moon.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-poe_moon-37)

[Jules Verne](/wiki/Jules_Verne "Jules Verne") was noted for his attention to detail and scientific accuracy, especially in the novel *[Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas](/wiki/Twenty_Thousand_Leagues_Under_the_Seas "Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas")* (1870).[[38]](#cite_note-Roberts48-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-thomas196112-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 1887, the novel *[El anacronópete](/wiki/El_anacron%C3%B3pete "El anacronópete")* by Spanish author [Enrique Gaspar y Rimbau](/wiki/Enrique_Gaspar_y_Rimbau "Enrique Gaspar y Rimbau") introduced the first [time machine](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) An early French/Belgian science fiction writer was [J.-H. Rosny aîné](/wiki/J.-H._Rosny_a%C3%AEn%C3%A9 "J.-H. Rosny aîné") (1856–1940). Rosny's masterpiece is *Les Navigateurs de l'Infini* (*The Navigators of Infinity*) (1925) in which the word *astronaut (astronautique* in French) was used for the first time.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg/250px-The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa%2C_original_graphic_15.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_War_of_the_Worlds_by_Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa,_original_graphic_15.jpg)

[Alien invasion](/wiki/Alien_invasion "Alien invasion") featured in the novel *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")* (1897) by [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), illustrated by [Henrique Alvim Corrêa](/wiki/Henrique_Alvim_Corr%C3%AAa "Henrique Alvim Corrêa") in 1906

Many critics consider H. G. Wells to be one of science fiction's most important authors,[[38]](#cite_note-Roberts48-38)[[46]](#cite_note-46) or even "the [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") of science fiction".[[47]](#cite_note-47) His novels include *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")* (1895), *[The Island of Doctor Moreau](/wiki/The_Island_of_Doctor_Moreau "The Island of Doctor Moreau")* (1896), *[The Invisible Man](/wiki/The_Invisible_Man "The Invisible Man")* (1897), and *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")* (1898). His science fiction imagined [alien invasion](/wiki/Alien_invasion "Alien invasion"), [biological engineering](/wiki/Biological_engineering "Biological engineering"), [invisibility](/wiki/Invisibility "Invisibility"), and [time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel"). In his [non-fiction](/wiki/Non-fiction "Non-fiction") [futurologist](/wiki/Futurologist "Futurologist") works, he predicted the advent of [airplanes](/wiki/Airplane "Airplane"), [military tanks](/wiki/Military_tank "Military tank"), [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"), [satellite television](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television"), [space travel](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight"), and something like the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[Edgar Rice Burroughs](/wiki/Edgar_Rice_Burroughs "Edgar Rice Burroughs")'s novel *[A Princess of Mars](/wiki/A_Princess_of_Mars "A Princess of Mars")*, published in 1912, was the first of his thirty-year [planetary romance](/wiki/Planetary_romance "Planetary romance") series about the fictional [Barsoom](/wiki/Barsoom "Barsoom"); the novels were set on Mars and featured [John Carter](/wiki/John_Carter_of_Mars "John Carter of Mars") as the [hero](/wiki/Hero "Hero").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica-50)

One of the first [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia") novels, [*We*](/wiki/We_(novel) "We (novel)"), was written by the Russian author [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin") and published in 1924.[[51]](#cite_note-51) It describes a world of harmony and conformity within a united [totalitarian state](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Science_and_Invention_no._124_%28August_1923%29.jpg/250px-Science_and_Invention_no._124_%28August_1923%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Science_and_Invention_no._124_(August_1923).jpg)

The August 1923 issue of *[Science and Invention](/wiki/Science_and_Invention "Science and Invention")* — the "Scientific Fiction number" — was the first issue of a Gernsback-published magazine to have significant fiction content.[[53]](#cite_note-Minnesota-53)

In 1926, [Hugo Gernsback](/wiki/Hugo_Gernsback "Hugo Gernsback") published the first American [science fiction magazine](/wiki/Science_fiction_magazine "Science fiction magazine"), *[Amazing Stories](/wiki/Amazing_Stories "Amazing Stories")*. In its first issue, he provided the definition:

> By 'scientifiction' I mean the Jules Verne, H. G. Wells and Edgar Allan Poe type of story—a charming romance intermingled with scientific fact and prophetic vision... Not only do these amazing tales make tremendously interesting reading—they are always instructive. They supply knowledge... in a very palatable form... New adventures pictured for us in the scientifiction of today are not at all impossible of realization tomorrow... Many great science stories destined to be of historical interest are still to be written... Posterity will point to them as having blazed a new trail, not only in literature and fiction, but progress as well.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-stableford-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 1928, [E. E. "Doc" Smith](/wiki/E._E._%22Doc%22_Smith "E. E. \"Doc\" Smith")'s first published novel, *[The Skylark of Space](/wiki/The_Skylark_of_Space "The Skylark of Space")* (co-authored with [Lee Hawkins Garby](/wiki/Lee_Hawkins_Garby "Lee Hawkins Garby")), appeared in *Amazing Stories*. It is often described as the first great [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera").[[57]](#cite_note-Dozois-57) That same year, [Philip Francis Nowlan](/wiki/Philip_Francis_Nowlan "Philip Francis Nowlan")'s original story about [Buck Rogers](/wiki/Buck_Rogers "Buck Rogers"), *[Armageddon 2419](/wiki/Armageddon_2419 "Armageddon 2419")*, also appeared in *Amazing Stories*. This story was followed by a Buck Rogers [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip"), the first serious [science fiction comic](/wiki/Science_fiction_comics "Science fiction comics").[[58]](#cite_note-guide-58)

*[Last and First Men: A Story of the Near and Far Future](/wiki/Last_and_First_Men:_A_Story_of_the_Near_and_Far_Future "Last and First Men: A Story of the Near and Far Future")* is a [future history](/wiki/Future_history "Future history") novel written in 1930 by the British author [Olaf Stapledon](/wiki/Olaf_Stapledon "Olaf Stapledon"). A work of innovative scale in the science fiction genre, it describes the fictional history of humanity from the present forward across two billion years.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 1937, [John W. Campbell](/wiki/John_W._Campbell "John W. Campbell") became the editor of *[Astounding Science Fiction](/wiki/Astounding_Science_Fiction "Astounding Science Fiction")* magazine; this event is sometimes considered the beginning of the [Golden Age of Science Fiction](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Science_Fiction "Golden Age of Science Fiction"), which was characterized by stories celebrating scientific achievement and progress.[[60]](#cite_note-sf_history_nvcc-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) The "Golden Age" is often said to have ended in 1946, but sometimes the late 1940s and the 1950s are included in this period.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Amazing_stories_192809.jpg/250px-Amazing_stories_192809.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amazing_stories_192809.jpg)

Amazing stories, Sept. 1928. [Hugo Gernsback](/wiki/Hugo_Gernsback "Hugo Gernsback") coined the term "Scientiction" in 1916.

In 1942, [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") began the [Foundation](/wiki/Foundation_(book_series) "Foundation (book series)") series of novels, which chronicles the rise and fall of galactic empires, and also introduces the concept of [*psychohistory*](/wiki/Psychohistory_(fictional) "Psychohistory (fictional)").[[63]](#cite_note-From_Robots_to_Foundations-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) The series was later awarded a one-time [Hugo Award](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award") for "Best All-Time Series".[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[Theodore Sturgeon](/wiki/Theodore_Sturgeon "Theodore Sturgeon")'s novel *[More Than Human](/wiki/More_Than_Human "More Than Human")* (1953) explored possible future [human evolution](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution").[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) In 1957, the novel *[Andromeda: A Space-Age Tale](/wiki/Andromeda:_A_Space-Age_Tale "Andromeda: A Space-Age Tale")* by the [Russian](/wiki/Russians "Russians") writer and [paleontologist](/wiki/Paleontologist "Paleontologist") [Ivan Yefremov](/wiki/Ivan_Yefremov "Ivan Yefremov") presented a view of a future interstellar [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") civilization; it is considered one of the most important Soviet science fiction novels.[[70]](#cite_note-sps-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In 1959, [Robert A. Heinlein](/wiki/Robert_A._Heinlein "Robert A. Heinlein")'s novel *[Starship Troopers](/wiki/Starship_Troopers "Starship Troopers")* marked a departure from his earlier juvenile stories and novels.[[72]](#cite_note-gale196010-72) It is one of the first and most influential examples of [military science fiction](/wiki/Military_science_fiction "Military science fiction"),[[73]](#cite_note-Mcmilllan-73)[[74]](#cite_note-Liptak-74) and it introduced the concept of [powered armor](/wiki/Powered_armor "Powered armor") [exoskeletons](/wiki/Exoskeleton "Exoskeleton").[[75]](#cite_note-Intersections-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) The German space opera series *[Perry Rhodan](/wiki/Perry_Rhodan "Perry Rhodan")*, written by various authors, started in 1961 with an account of the first [Moon landing](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing");[[78]](#cite_note-78) the series has since expanded in space to multiple universes and in time by billions of years.[[79]](#cite_note-79) It has become the most popular book series in science fiction to date.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

During the 1960s and 1970s, [New Wave](/wiki/New_Wave_science_fiction "New Wave science fiction") science fiction was known for embracing a high degree of experimentation (in both form and content), as well as a highbrow and self-consciously "literary" or "artistic" sensibility.[[81]](#cite_note-McGuirk-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In 1961, [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem")'s novel *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(novel) "Solaris (novel)")* was published in Poland.[[83]](#cite_note-83) The novel dealt with the [theme](/wiki/Theme_(arts) "Theme (arts)") of human limitations, as its characters attempted to study a seemingly intelligent ocean on a newly discovered planet.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Lem's work anticipated the creation of [microrobots](/wiki/Microrobotics "Microrobotics") and [micromachinery](/wiki/Micromachinery "Micromachinery"), [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), [smartdust](/wiki/Smartdust "Smartdust"), [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality"), and [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (including [swarm intelligence](/wiki/Swarm_intelligence "Swarm intelligence")); his work also developed the ideas of *necroevolution* and artificial worlds.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

In 1965, the novel *[Dune](/wiki/Dune_(novel) "Dune (novel)")* by [Frank Herbert](/wiki/Frank_Herbert "Frank Herbert") imagined a more complex and detailed future society than had most previous science fiction.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In 1967, [Anne McCaffrey](/wiki/Anne_McCaffrey "Anne McCaffrey") began a [science fantasy](/wiki/Science_fantasy "Science fantasy") series called *[Dragonriders of Pern](/wiki/Dragonriders_of_Pern "Dragonriders of Pern")*.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Two novellas included in the series' first novel, *[Dragonflight](/wiki/Dragonflight_(novel) "Dragonflight (novel)")*, led McCaffrey to win the first [Hugo](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award") or [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula_Award "Nebula Award") award given to a female author.[[92]](#cite_note-first-92)

In 1968, [Philip K. Dick](/wiki/Philip_K._Dick "Philip K. Dick")'s novel *[Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?](/wiki/Do_Androids_Dream_of_Electric_Sheep%3F "Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?")* was published. It is the literary source of the *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner_(franchise) "Blade Runner (franchise)")* [movie franchise](/wiki/Movie_franchise "Movie franchise").[[93]](#cite_note-Sammon-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) Published in 1969, the novel *[The Left Hand of Darkness](/wiki/The_Left_Hand_of_Darkness "The Left Hand of Darkness")* by [Ursula K. Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin") is set on a planet where the inhabitants have no fixed gender. The novel is one of the most influential examples of [social](/wiki/Social_science_fiction "Social science fiction"), [feminist](/wiki/Feminist_science_fiction "Feminist science fiction"), or [anthropological](/wiki/Anthropological_science_fiction "Anthropological science fiction") science fiction.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

In 1979, *[Science Fiction World](/wiki/Science_Fiction_World "Science Fiction World")* magazine began publication in the People's Republic of China.[[98]](#cite_note-98) It dominates the Chinese [science fiction magazine](/wiki/Science_fiction_magazine "Science fiction magazine") market, at one time claiming a circulation of 300,000 copies per issue and an estimated 3–5 readers per copy, giving it a total readership of at least 1 million people—making it the world's most popular science fiction [periodical](/wiki/Periodical_literature "Periodical literature").[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In 1984, [William Gibson](/wiki/William_Gibson "William Gibson")'s first novel, *[Neuromancer](/wiki/Neuromancer "Neuromancer")*, helped to popularize [cyberpunk](/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") and the word *[cyberspace](/wiki/Cyberspace "Cyberspace")*, a term he originally coined in the 1982 [short story](/wiki/Short_story "Short story") *[Burning Chrome](/wiki/Burning_Chrome "Burning Chrome")*.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-gibson_cyber-102) In the same year, [Octavia Butler](/wiki/Octavia_E._Butler "Octavia E. Butler")'s short story "[Speech Sounds](/wiki/Speech_Sounds "Speech Sounds")" won the Hugo Award for Best Short Story. She went on to explore themes of racial injustice, global warming, women's rights, and political conflict.[[103]](#cite_note-103) In 1995, she became the first science fiction author to receive a [MacArthur Fellowship](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellowship "MacArthur Fellowship").[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In 1986, the novel *[Shards of Honor](/wiki/Shards_of_Honor "Shards of Honor")* by [Lois McMaster Bujold](/wiki/Lois_McMaster_Bujold "Lois McMaster Bujold") began her [Vorkosigan Saga](/wiki/Vorkosigan_Saga "Vorkosigan Saga") science opera.[[105]](#cite_note-Tor_Shards-105)[[106]](#cite_note-Kelso-106) 1992's novel *[Snow Crash](/wiki/Snow_Crash "Snow Crash")* by [Neal Stephenson](/wiki/Neal_Stephenson "Neal Stephenson") predicted immense social upheaval due to the [information revolution](/wiki/Information_revolution "Information revolution").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2007, [Liu Cixin](/wiki/Liu_Cixin "Liu Cixin")'s novel *[The Three-Body Problem](/wiki/The_Three-Body_Problem_(novel) "The Three-Body Problem (novel)")* was published in China. It was translated into English by [Ken Liu](/wiki/Ken_Liu "Ken Liu") and published by [Tor Books](/wiki/Tor_Books "Tor Books") in 2014;[[108]](#cite_note-108) it won the [Hugo Award for Best Novel](/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Novel "Hugo Award for Best Novel") in 2015,[[109]](#cite_note-109) making Liu the first Asian writer to win the award.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Emerging themes in late 20th- and early 21st-century science fiction include [environmental issues](/wiki/List_of_environmental_issues "List of environmental issues"), the implications of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") and the expanding information universe, questions about [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), and [post-scarcity](/wiki/Post-scarcity "Post-scarcity") [societies](/wiki/Societies "Societies").[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) Recent trends and [subgenres](/wiki/Subgenres "Subgenres") include [steampunk](/wiki/Steampunk "Steampunk"),[[113]](#cite_note-113) [biopunk](/wiki/Biopunk "Biopunk"),[[114]](#cite_note-Pulver_1998-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115) and [mundane](/wiki/Mundane_science_fiction "Mundane science fiction") science fiction.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-dwalter-117)

## Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Film")]

Main articles: [Science fiction film](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") and [Lists of science fiction films](/wiki/Lists_of_science_fiction_films "Lists of science fiction films")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Maria_from_the_film_Metropolis%2C_on_display_at_the_Robot_Hall_of_Fame.jpg/250px-Maria_from_the_film_Metropolis%2C_on_display_at_the_Robot_Hall_of_Fame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_from_the_film_Metropolis,_on_display_at_the_Robot_Hall_of_Fame.jpg)

The [Maschinenmensch](/wiki/Maschinenmensch "Maschinenmensch") (or machine-human) from *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* (1927)

One of the first recorded science fiction [films](/wiki/Film "Film") is *[A Trip to the Moon](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")* from 1902, directed by French [filmmaker](/wiki/Filmmaker "Filmmaker") [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès").[[118]](#cite_note-Dixon12-118) It influenced later filmmakers, offering a different kind of [creativity](/wiki/Creativity "Creativity") and [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy").[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120) Méliès's innovative [editing](/wiki/Editing "Editing") and [special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect") techniques were widely imitated, and they became important elements of the cinematic [medium](/wiki/Media_(communication) "Media (communication)").[[121]](#cite_note-1001Movies-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

The 1927 film *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")*, directed by [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang"), is the first [feature-length](/wiki/Feature-length "Feature-length") science fiction film.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Though not well received in its time,[[124]](#cite_note-124) it is now ranked as one of the best films ever made.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-bfi-127)

In 1954, *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1954_film) "Godzilla (1954 film)")*, directed by [Ishirō Honda](/wiki/Ishir%C5%8D_Honda "Ishirō Honda"), started the [kaiju](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju") [subgenre](/wiki/Subgenre "Subgenre") of science fiction film; this subgenre features large creatures in any form, usually attacking a major city or engaging other [monsters](/wiki/Monster "Monster") in battle.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)

The 1968 film *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")*, was directed by [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") and based on
stories by [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke"). The film improved on the largely [B-movie](/wiki/B-movie "B-movie") offerings to date in both scope and quality, and it influenced later science fiction films.[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)

The original *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(1968_film) "Planet of the Apes (1968 film)")* movie, directed by [Franklin J. Schaffner](/wiki/Franklin_J._Schaffner "Franklin J. Schaffner") and based on the 1963 French novel *[La Planète des Singes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(novel) "Planet of the Apes (novel)")* by [Pierre Boulle](/wiki/Pierre_Boulle "Pierre Boulle"), was also released in 1968. The film vividly depicts a [post-apocalyptic](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction") world in which intelligent apes dominate humans.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The film received both popular and critical acclaim.

In 1977, [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas") began the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* series with the film later called "*[Star Wars: Episode IV – A New Hope](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)").*"[[135]](#cite_note-135) The series, often called a space opera,[[136]](#cite_note-136) became a worldwide [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture") phenomenon[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138) and the [third-highest-grossing](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_franchises_and_film_series "List of highest-grossing franchises and film series") film series of all time.[[139]](#cite_note-boxofficemojo.com-139)

Since the 1980s, [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") films, along with [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy"), [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film"), and [superhero](/wiki/Superhero "Superhero") films, have dominated [Hollywood's](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") big-budget productions.[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[139]](#cite_note-boxofficemojo.com-139) Science fiction films often [*cross ove*r](/wiki/Cross-genre "Cross-genre") with other genres. Some examples include [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") (*[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")*, 1982), [family](/wiki/Children%27s_film "Children's film") (*[E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial](/wiki/E.T._the_Extra-Terrestrial "E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial")*, 1982), [war](/wiki/War_film "War film") (*[Enemy Mine](/wiki/Enemy_Mine_(film) "Enemy Mine (film)")*, 1985), [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") (*[Spaceballs](/wiki/Spaceballs "Spaceballs") , 1987;* [Galaxy Quest](/wiki/Galaxy_Quest "Galaxy Quest"), 1999), [animation](/wiki/Animation "Animation") *([WALL-E](/wiki/WALL-E "WALL-E")*, 2008; *[Big Hero 6](/wiki/Big_Hero_6_(film) "Big Hero 6 (film)")*, 2014), [Western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") (*[Serenity](/wiki/Serenity_(2005_film) "Serenity (2005 film)")*, 2005), [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") (*[Edge of Tomorrow](/wiki/Edge_of_Tomorrow "Edge of Tomorrow")*, 2014; *[The Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")*, 1999), [adventure](/wiki/Adventure_film "Adventure film") (*[Jupiter Ascending](/wiki/Jupiter_Ascending "Jupiter Ascending")*, 2015; *[Interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_(film) "Interstellar (film)")*, 2014), [mystery](/wiki/Mystery_film "Mystery film") (*[Minority Report](/wiki/Minority_Report_(film) "Minority Report (film)")*, 2002), [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") (*[Ex Machina](/wiki/Ex_Machina_(film) "Ex Machina (film)")*, 2014), [drama](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)") (*[Melancholia](/wiki/Melancholia_(2011_film) "Melancholia (2011 film)")*, 2011; *[Predestination](/wiki/Predestination_(film) "Predestination (film)")*, 2014), and [romance](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film") (*[Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind](/wiki/Eternal_Sunshine_of_the_Spotless_Mind "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind")*, 2004; *[Her](/wiki/Her_(2013_film) "Her (2013 film)")*, 2013).[[141]](#cite_note-JohnsonSFF-141)

## Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Television")]

Main articles: [Science fiction on television](/wiki/Science_fiction_on_television "Science fiction on television") and [List of science fiction television programs](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_television_programs "List of science fiction television programs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Al_Hodge_Don_Hastings_Captain_Video.JPG/250px-Al_Hodge_Don_Hastings_Captain_Video.JPG)](/wiki/File:Al_Hodge_Don_Hastings_Captain_Video.JPG)

Don Hastings (left) and Al Hodge (right) in *[Captain Video and His Video Rangers](/wiki/Captain_Video_and_His_Video_Rangers "Captain Video and His Video Rangers")*

Science fiction and [television](/wiki/Television "Television") have consistently had a close relationship. Television or similar [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology") often appeared in science fiction before television itself became widely available in the late 1940s and early 1950s.[[142]](#cite_note-Telotte-142)

The first known science fiction television program was a 35-minute [adapted](/wiki/Film_adaptation "Film adaptation") excerpt of the play *[RUR](/wiki/R.U.R._(Rossum%27s_Universal_Robots) "R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)")*, written by the Czech playwright [Karel Čapek](/wiki/Karel_%C4%8Capek "Karel Čapek"), broadcast live from the BBC's [Alexandra Palace](/wiki/Alexandra_Palace "Alexandra Palace") studios on 11 February 1938.[[143]](#cite_note-r.u.r.-143) The first popular science fiction program on [American television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_States "Television in the United States") was the [children's](/wiki/Children%27s_television_series "Children's television series") adventure [serial](/wiki/Serial_(radio_and_television) "Serial (radio and television)") *[Captain Video and His Video Rangers](/wiki/Captain_Video_and_His_Video_Rangers "Captain Video and His Video Rangers")*, which ran from June 1949 to April 1955.[[144]](#cite_note-cpt_video-144)

The original *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1959_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1959 TV series)")* series, produced and narrated by [Rod Serling](/wiki/Rod_Serling "Rod Serling"), ran from 1959 to 1964. (Serling also wrote or co-wrote most of the episodes.) The series featured [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy"), [suspense](/wiki/Suspense_(genre) "Suspense (genre)"), and [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") as well as science fiction, with each episode being a complete story.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) [Critics](/wiki/Critic "Critic") have ranked it as one of the best [TV programs](/wiki/TV_programs "TV programs") of any [genre](/wiki/Genre "Genre").[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)

The [animated](/wiki/Animation "Animation") series *[The Jetsons](/wiki/The_Jetsons "The Jetsons")*, while intended as [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") and only running for one [season](/wiki/Season_(television) "Season (television)") (1962–1963), predicted many inventions now in common use: [flat-screen](/wiki/Flat_panel_display "Flat panel display") [televisions](/wiki/Television "Television"), newspapers on a [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer")-like [screen](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), [computer viruses](/wiki/Computer_virus "Computer virus"), [video chat](/wiki/Videotelephony "Videotelephony"), [tanning beds](/wiki/Tanning_bed "Tanning bed"), home [treadmills](/wiki/Treadmill "Treadmill"), and more.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

In 1963, the series *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* premiered on BBC Television with a time-travel theme.[[150]](#cite_note-150) The original series ran until 1989 and was revived in 2005.[[151]](#cite_note-151) It has been popular globally and has significantly influenced later science fiction TV.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-Moran-154)

Other British sci-fi dramas which are broadcast in the 1970s are *[UFO](/wiki/UFO_(British_TV_series) "UFO (British TV series)")* (1970–1971), *[The Tomorrow People](/wiki/The_Tomorrow_People "The Tomorrow People")* (1973–1979), *[Space: 1999](/wiki/Space:_1999 "Space: 1999")* (1975–1977) and *[Blake's 7](/wiki/Blake%27s_7 "Blake's 7")* (1978–1981). Other notable programs during the 1960s included *[The Outer Limits](/wiki/The_Outer_Limits_(1963_TV_series) "The Outer Limits (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1965),[[155]](#cite_note-155) *[Lost in Space](/wiki/Lost_in_Space "Lost in Space")* (1965–1968), and *[The Prisoner](/wiki/The_Prisoner "The Prisoner")* (1967–1968).[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)

The original *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series")* series, created by [Gene Roddenberry](/wiki/Gene_Roddenberry "Gene Roddenberry"), premiered in 1966 on [NBC Television](/wiki/NBC_Television "NBC Television") and ran for three seasons.[[159]](#cite_note-159) It combined elements of [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera") and [Space Western](/wiki/Space_Western "Space Western").[[160]](#cite_note-160) Only mildly successful at first, the series gained popularity through [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") and strong [fan interest](/wiki/Cultural_influence_of_Star_Trek#Fandom "Cultural influence of Star Trek"). It became a popular and influential [franchise](/wiki/Star_Trek_franchise "Star Trek franchise") with many [films](/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_films "List of Star Trek films"), [television shows](/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_television_series "List of Star Trek television series"), [novels](/wiki/List_of_Star_Trek_novels "List of Star Trek novels"), and other works and products.[[161]](#cite_note-STPitch1-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164) The series *[Star Trek: The Next Generation](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation "Star Trek: The Next Generation")* (1987–1994) led to six additional live action *Star Trek* shows: *[Deep Space Nine](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Deep_Space_Nine "Star Trek: Deep Space Nine")* (1993–1999), *[Voyager](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Voyager "Star Trek: Voyager")* (1995–2001)*,* *[Enterprise](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Enterprise "Star Trek: Enterprise")* (2001–2005), *[Discovery](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Discovery "Star Trek: Discovery")* (2017–2024), *[Picard](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Picard "Star Trek: Picard")* (2020–2023), and *[Strange New Worlds](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Strange_New_Worlds "Star Trek: Strange New Worlds")* (2022–present); additional shows are in some stage of development.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168)

The [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") [*V*](/wiki/V_(1983_miniseries) "V (1983 miniseries)") premiered in 1983 on NBC.[[169]](#cite_note-169) It depicted an attempted conquest of Earth by [reptilian aliens](/wiki/Reptilian_aliens "Reptilian aliens").[[170]](#cite_note-EW_2005-170) *[Red Dwarf](/wiki/Red_Dwarf "Red Dwarf")*, a [comic](/wiki/Comic_science_fiction "Comic science fiction") science fiction series, aired on [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") between 1988 and 1999, and on [Dave](/wiki/Dave_(TV_channel) "Dave (TV channel)") since 2009.[[171]](#cite_note-171) *[The X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")*, which featured [UFOs](/wiki/UFO "UFO") and [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories"), was created by [Chris Carter](/wiki/Chris_Carter_(screenwriter) "Chris Carter (screenwriter)") and broadcast by [Fox Broadcasting Company](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") from 1993 to 2002,[[172]](#cite_note-BehindTheXFiles-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173) and again from 2016 to 2018.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)

*[Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(film) "Stargate (film)")*, a film about [ancient astronauts](/wiki/Ancient_astronauts "Ancient astronauts") and interstellar [teleportation](/wiki/Teleportation "Teleportation"), was released in 1994. The series *[Stargate SG-1](/wiki/Stargate_SG-1 "Stargate SG-1")* premiered in 1997 and ran for 10 seasons (1997–2007). Spin-off series included *[Stargate Infinity](/wiki/Stargate_Infinity "Stargate Infinity")* (2002–2003), *[Stargate Atlantis](/wiki/Stargate_Atlantis "Stargate Atlantis")* (2004–2009), and *[Stargate Universe](/wiki/Stargate_Universe "Stargate Universe")* (2009–2011).[[176]](#cite_note-176)

Other 1990s series included *[Quantum Leap](/wiki/Quantum_Leap_(1989_TV_series) "Quantum Leap (1989 TV series)")* (1989–1993) and *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")* (1994–1999).[[177]](#cite_note-177) The [Syfy](/wiki/Syfy "Syfy") channel, launched in 1992 as The Sci-Fi Channel,[[178]](#cite_note-178) specializes in science fiction, [supernatural horror](/wiki/Supernatural_horror "Supernatural horror"), and [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy").[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)

The space-Western series *[Firefly](/wiki/Firefly_(TV_series) "Firefly (TV series)")* premiered in 2002 on Fox. It is set in the year 2517, after humans arrive in a new star system, and it follows the adventures of the renegade crew of *[Serenity](/wiki/Serenity_(fictional_spacecraft) "Serenity (fictional spacecraft)")*, a "*Firefly*-class" spaceship.[[181]](#cite_note-torontosun-181) The series *[Orphan Black](/wiki/Orphan_Black "Orphan Black")* began a five-season run in 2013, focusing on a woman who takes on the identity of one of her genetically identical clones. In late 2015, Syfy premiered the series *[The Expanse](/wiki/The_Expanse_(TV_series) "The Expanse (TV series)")* to great critical acclaim—an American show about humanity's colonization of the Solar System. Its later seasons were aired through [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video").

## Social influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Social influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Imagination_195808.jpg/250px-Imagination_195808.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imagination_195808.jpg)

[Space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration") was predicted in August 1958 by the [science fiction magazine](/wiki/Science_fiction_magazine "Science fiction magazine") *[Imagination](/wiki/Imagination_(magazine) "Imagination (magazine)")*.

Science fiction's rapid increase in popularity during the first half of the 20th century was closely tied to public respect for science during that era, as well as the rapid pace of [technological innovation](/wiki/Technological_innovation "Technological innovation") and new [inventions](/wiki/Invention "Invention").[[182]](#cite_note-AWonder-182) Science fiction has often predicted scientific and technological progress.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) Some works imagine that this progress will tend to improve human life and society, for instance, the stories of [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke") and *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")*.[[185]](#cite_note-185) Other works, such as [H.G. Wells's](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")* and [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley")'s *[Brave New World](/wiki/Brave_New_World "Brave New World")*, warn of possible negative consequences.[[186]](#cite_note-Greenwood-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187)

In 2001 the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") conducted a [survey](/wiki/Survey_(human_research) "Survey (human research)") of "Public Attitudes and Public Understanding: Science Fiction and [Pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience")".[[188]](#cite_note-NSF-188) The survey found that people who read or prefer science fiction may think about or relate to science differently than other people. Such people also tend to support the [space program](/wiki/Space_program "Space program") and efforts to contact [extraterrestrial civilizations](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life "Extraterrestrial life").[[188]](#cite_note-NSF-188)[[189]](#cite_note-189) [Carl Sagan](/wiki/Carl_Sagan "Carl Sagan") wrote that "Many scientists deeply involved in the exploration of the [solar system](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") (myself among them) were first turned in that direction by science fiction."[[190]](#cite_note-growing-190)

Science fiction has [predicted several existing inventions](/wiki/List_of_existing_technologies_predicted_in_science_fiction "List of existing technologies predicted in science fiction"), such as the [atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_bomb "Atomic bomb"),[[191]](#cite_note-191) [robots](/wiki/Robots "Robots"),[[192]](#cite_note-192) and [borazon](/wiki/Borazon "Borazon").[[193]](#cite_note-193) In the 2020 TV series *[Away](/wiki/Away_(TV_series) "Away (TV series)"),* astronauts use a Mars rover called [InSight](/wiki/InSight "InSight") to listen for a landing on [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"). In 2022, scientists actually used InSight to listen for the landing of a [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft").[[194]](#cite_note-194)

Science fiction can act as a vehicle for analyzing and recognizing a society's past, present, and potential future social relationships with the [other](/wiki/Other_(philosophy) "Other (philosophy)"). Science fiction offers a medium for and a representation of [alterity](/wiki/Alterity "Alterity") and differences in [social identity](/wiki/Identity_(social_science) "Identity (social science)").[[195]](#cite_note-195) [Brian Aldiss](/wiki/Brian_Aldiss "Brian Aldiss") described science fiction as "cultural wallpaper".[[196]](#cite_note-196)

This broad influence can be seen in the trend for writers to use science fiction as a tool for advocacy and generating cultural insights, as well as for educators who teach across a range of academic disciplines beyond the natural sciences.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Scholar and science fiction critic [George Edgar Slusser](/wiki/George_Edgar_Slusser "George Edgar Slusser") said that science fiction "is the one real international [literary form](/wiki/Literary_form "Literary form") we have today, and as such has branched out to [visual media](/wiki/Visual_media "Visual media"), [interactive media](/wiki/Interactive_media "Interactive media") and on to whatever new media the world will invent in the 21st century. Crossover issues between the [sciences](/wiki/Science "Science") and the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") are crucial for the century to come."[[198]](#cite_note-198)

### As protest literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: As protest literature")]

Further information: [Social novel](/wiki/Social_novel "Social novel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Feliz_1984.JPG/250px-Feliz_1984.JPG)](/wiki/File:Feliz_1984.JPG)

"Happy 1984" in Spanish or Portuguese, referencing [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s novel *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four")*, on a standing piece of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall") (sometime after 1998)

Science fiction has sometimes been used as a means of [social protest](/wiki/Social_protest "Social protest"). [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s novel *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four")* (1949) is an important work of [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian_fiction "Dystopian fiction") science fiction.[[199]](#cite_note-BenetReader-199)[[200]](#cite_note-aaron-200) The novel is often invoked in protests against governments and leaders who are seen as [totalitarian](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism").[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202) [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron")'s film *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")* (2009) was intended as a protest against [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism"), specifically the [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas").[[203]](#cite_note-npr-203) Science fiction in Latin America and Spain explores the concept of [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism").[[204]](#cite_note-204)

[Robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot"), [artificial humans](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"), human [clones](/wiki/Cloning "Cloning"), intelligent [computers](/wiki/Computers "Computers"), and their possible conflicts with human society have all been major themes of science fiction since the publication of Shelly's novel *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* (or earlier). Some critics have seen this tendency as reflecting authors' concerns over the [social alienation](/wiki/Social_alienation "Social alienation") seen in modern society.[[205]](#cite_note-Schelde1994-205)

[Feminist](/wiki/Feminist_science_fiction "Feminist science fiction") science fiction poses questions about social issues such as how society constructs [gender roles](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role"), the role reproduction plays in defining [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"), and the inequitable political or personal power of one gender over others. Some works have illustrated these themes using [utopias](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") in which gender differences or gender power imbalances do not exist, or [dystopias](/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia") in which [gender inequalities](/wiki/Gender_inequality "Gender inequality") are intensified, thus asserting a need for feminist work to continue.[[206]](#cite_note-encyclopedia3-206)[[207]](#cite_note-207)

[Climate](/wiki/Climate_fiction "Climate fiction") fiction (or *cli-fi*) deals with issues of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_variability_and_change "Climate variability and change") and [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming").[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-DanBloom-209) University courses on [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") and [environmental issues](/wiki/Environmental_issue "Environmental issue") may include climate change fiction in their [syllabi](/wiki/Syllabus "Syllabus"),[[210]](#cite_note-210) and these issues are often discussed by other [media](/wiki/Media_(communication) "Media (communication)") beyond science fiction [fandom](/wiki/Science_fiction_fandom "Science fiction fandom").[[211]](#cite_note-211)

[Libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian_science_fiction "Libertarian science fiction") science fiction focuses on the [politics](/wiki/Politics "Politics") and [social order](/wiki/Social_order "Social order") implied by [right libertarian](/wiki/Right_libertarian "Right libertarian") philosophies with an emphasis on [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism") and [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property"), and in some cases [anti-statism](/wiki/Anti-statism "Anti-statism").[[212]](#cite_note-Raymond-212) [Robert A. Heinlein](/wiki/Robert_A._Heinlein "Robert A. Heinlein") is one of the most popular authors of this subgenre, including his novels *[The Moon is a Harsh Mistress](/wiki/The_Moon_Is_a_Harsh_Mistress "The Moon Is a Harsh Mistress")* and *[Stranger in a Strange Land](/wiki/Stranger_in_a_Strange_Land "Stranger in a Strange Land")*.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

Science fiction [comedy](/wiki/Science_fiction_comedy "Science fiction comedy") often [satirizes](/wiki/Satire "Satire") and [criticizes](/wiki/Criticism "Criticism") present-day society, and it sometimes makes fun of the [conventions](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)") and [clichés](/wiki/Clich%C3%A9 "Cliché") of more serious science fiction.[[214]](#cite_note-Fantasy,_Bruce_Shaw_2010,_page_19-214)[[215]](#cite_note-Comedy_Science_Fiction-215)

### Sense of wonder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sense of wonder")]

Main article: [Sense of wonder](/wiki/Sense_of_wonder "Sense of wonder")

Further information: [Wonder (emotion)](/wiki/Wonder_(emotion) "Wonder (emotion)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/William_Strang_spider_battle_in_1894_True_History.svg/250px-William_Strang_spider_battle_in_1894_True_History.svg.png)](/wiki/File:William_Strang_spider_battle_in_1894_True_History.svg)

1894 illustration by [Aubrey Beardsley](/wiki/Aubrey_Beardsley "Aubrey Beardsley") for [Lucian](/wiki/Lucian "Lucian")'s novel *[A True Story](/wiki/A_True_Story "A True Story")*

Science fiction is often said to inspire a *[sense of wonder](/wiki/Sense_of_wonder "Sense of wonder")*. Science fiction editor, publisher, and critic [David Hartwell](/wiki/David_Hartwell "David Hartwell") wrote that "Science fiction's appeal lies in combination of the rational, the believable, with the miraculous. It is an appeal to the sense of wonder."[[216]](#cite_note-216)

Carl Sagan wrote about growing up with science fiction:[[190]](#cite_note-growing-190)

> One of the great benefits of science fiction is that it can convey bits and pieces, hints, and phrases, of knowledge unknown or inaccessible to the reader . . . works you ponder over as the water is running out of the bathtub or as you walk through the woods in an early winter snowfall.

In 1967, Isaac Asimov commented on changes occurring in the science fiction community:[[217]](#cite_note-217)

> And because today's real life so resembles day-before-yesterday's fantasy, the old-time fans are restless. Deep within, whether they admit it or not, is a feeling of disappointment and even outrage that the outer world has invaded their private domain. They feel the loss of a 'sense of wonder' because what was once truly confined to 'wonder' has now become prosaic and mundane.

## Study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Study")]

Main article: [Science fiction studies](/wiki/Science_fiction_studies "Science fiction studies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Victoria_Building%2C_University_of_Liverpool_2019.jpg/250px-Victoria_Building%2C_University_of_Liverpool_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Building,_University_of_Liverpool_2019.jpg)

The centrepiece of the university estate, the [Victoria Building, University of Liverpool](/wiki/Victoria_Building,_University_of_Liverpool "Victoria Building, University of Liverpool"), as a science fiction degree-granting program.

The field of science fiction [studies](/wiki/Science_fiction_studies "Science fiction studies") involves the [critical](/wiki/Criticism "Criticism") assessment, [interpretation](/wiki/Aesthetic_interpretation "Aesthetic interpretation"), and [discussion](/wiki/Conversation "Conversation") of science fiction [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), [film](/wiki/Film "Film"), [TV shows](/wiki/TV_shows "TV shows"), [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media"), [fandom](/wiki/Fandom "Fandom"), and [fan fiction](/wiki/Fan_fiction "Fan fiction").[[218]](#cite_note-218) Science fiction [scholars](/wiki/Scholar "Scholar") study the genre to better understand it and its relationship to science, technology, politics, other genres, and culture at large.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

Science fiction studies began around the turn of the 20th century, but it was not until later that science fiction studies solidified as a discipline with the publication of the academic journals *[Extrapolation](/wiki/Extrapolation_(journal) "Extrapolation (journal)")* (1959), *[Foundation: The International Review of Science Fiction](https://foundation.wikimedia.org/wiki/The_International_Review_of_Science_Fiction "foundation:The International Review of Science Fiction")* (1972), and *[Science Fiction Studies](/wiki/Science_Fiction_Studies "Science Fiction Studies")* (1973),[[220]](#cite_note-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221) and the establishment of the oldest organizations devoted to the [study](/wiki/Research "Research") of science fiction in 1970, the [Science Fiction Research Association](/wiki/Science_Fiction_Research_Association "Science Fiction Research Association") and the [Science Fiction Foundation](/wiki/Science_Fiction_Foundation "Science Fiction Foundation").[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223) The field has grown considerably since the 1970s with the establishment of more [journals](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal"), [organizations](/wiki/Organization "Organization"), and [conferences](/wiki/Academic_conference "Academic conference"), as well as science fiction [degree](/wiki/Academic_degree "Academic degree")-granting programs such as those offered by the [University of Liverpool](/wiki/University_of_Liverpool "University of Liverpool").[[224]](#cite_note-224)

### Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Classification")]

Further information: [Hard science fiction](/wiki/Hard_science_fiction "Hard science fiction") and [Soft science fiction](/wiki/Soft_science_fiction "Soft science fiction")

Science fiction has historically been subdivided into [*hard*](/wiki/Hard_science_fiction "Hard science fiction") and [*soft*](/wiki/Soft_science_fiction "Soft science fiction") categories, with the division centering on the feasibility of the science.[[225]](#cite_note-225) However, this distinction has come under increased scrutiny in the 21st century. Some [authors](/wiki/Author "Author"), such as [Tade Thompson](/wiki/Tade_Thompson "Tade Thompson") and [Jeff VanderMeer](/wiki/Jeff_VanderMeer "Jeff VanderMeer"), have observed that stories focusing explicitly on [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") tend to be considered hard science fiction, while stories focusing on [botany](/wiki/Botany "Botany"), [mycology](/wiki/Mycology "Mycology"), [zoology](/wiki/Zoology "Zoology"), and the [social sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science") tend to be considered soft science fiction (regardless of the relative rigor of the science).[[226]](#cite_note-tor.com-226)

[Max Gladstone](/wiki/Max_Gladstone "Max Gladstone") defined hard science fiction as stories "where the [math](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") works", but he pointed out that this definition identifies stories that often seem "weirdly dated", as scientific [paradigms](/wiki/Paradigm "Paradigm") shift over time.[[227]](#cite_note-227) [Michael Swanwick](/wiki/Michael_Swanwick "Michael Swanwick") dismissed the traditional definition of hard science fiction altogether, instead stating that it was defined by characters striving to solve problems "in the right way–with determination, a touch of stoicism, and the consciousness that the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe") is not on his or her side."[[226]](#cite_note-tor.com-226)

[Ursula K. Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin") also criticized the traditional contrast between hard and soft science fiction: "The 'hard' science fiction writers dismiss everything except, well, physics, astronomy, and maybe [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"). [Biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology")—that's not [science](/wiki/Science "Science") to them, that's soft stuff. They're not that interested in what human beings do, really. But I am. I draw on the social sciences a great deal."[[228]](#cite_note-228)

### Literary merit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Literary merit")]

Further information: [Literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") and [Literary fiction](/wiki/Literary_fiction "Literary fiction")

[![Engraving showing a naked man awaking on the floor and another man fleeing in horror. A skull and a book are next to the naked man and a window, with the moon shining through it, is in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Frontispiece_to_Frankenstein_1831.jpg/250px-Frontispiece_to_Frankenstein_1831.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frontispiece_to_Frankenstein_1831.jpg)

Illustration by [Theodor von Holst](/wiki/Theodor_von_Holst "Theodor von Holst") for the 1831 edition of [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley")'s novel *Frankenstein*[[229]](#cite_note-229)

Many critics remain skeptical of the [literary value](/wiki/Literary_value "Literary value") of science fiction and other forms of [genre fiction](/wiki/Genre_fiction "Genre fiction"), though some mainstream authors have written works claimed by opponents to be science fiction. [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley") wrote a number of [scientific romance](/wiki/Scientific_romance "Scientific romance") novels in the [Gothic literature](/wiki/Gothic_literature "Gothic literature") tradition, including *[Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* (1818).[[230]](#cite_note-introduction-230) [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut") was a respected American author whose works have been argued by some to contain science fiction premises or themes.[[231]](#cite_note-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232)

Other science fiction authors whose works are widely considered to be "serious" literature include [Ray Bradbury](/wiki/Ray_Bradbury "Ray Bradbury") (especially *[Fahrenheit 451](/wiki/Fahrenheit_451 "Fahrenheit 451")* and *[The Martian Chronicles](/wiki/The_Martian_Chronicles "The Martian Chronicles")*),[[233]](#cite_note-NYT-20120606-233) [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke") (especially *[Childhood's End](/wiki/Childhood%27s_End "Childhood's End")*),[[234]](#cite_note-234)[[235]](#cite_note-235) and Paul Myron Anthony Linebarger (using the pseudonym [Cordwainer Smith](/wiki/Cordwainer_Smith "Cordwainer Smith")).[[236]](#cite_note-236) [Doris Lessing](/wiki/Doris_Lessing "Doris Lessing"), who was later awarded the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"), wrote a series of five science fiction novels, *[Canopus in Argos: Archives](/wiki/Canopus_in_Argos "Canopus in Argos")* (1979–1983); these novels depict the efforts of more advanced species and civilizations to influence less advanced ones, including humans on Earth.[[237]](#cite_note-Hazelton-237)[[238]](#cite_note-Galin-238)[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-Donoghue-240)

[David Barnett](/wiki/David_Barnett_(writer) "David Barnett (writer)") has indicated that some novels use recognizable science fiction [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(literature) "Trope (literature)"), but they are not classified by their authors and publishers as science fiction; such novels include *[The Road](/wiki/The_Road "The Road")* (2006) by [Cormac McCarthy](/wiki/Cormac_McCarthy "Cormac McCarthy"), *[Cloud Atlas](/wiki/Cloud_Atlas_(novel) "Cloud Atlas (novel)")* (2004) by [David Mitchell](/wiki/David_Mitchell_(author) "David Mitchell (author)"), *[The Gone-Away World](/wiki/The_Gone-Away_World "The Gone-Away World")* (2008) by [Nick Harkaway](/wiki/Nick_Harkaway "Nick Harkaway"), *[The Stone Gods](/wiki/The_Stone_Gods_(novel) "The Stone Gods (novel)")* (2007) by [Jeanette Winterson](/wiki/Jeanette_Winterson "Jeanette Winterson"), and *[Oryx and Crake](/wiki/Oryx_and_Crake "Oryx and Crake")* (2003) by [Margaret Atwood](/wiki/Margaret_Atwood "Margaret Atwood").[[241]](#cite_note-guardian4-241) Atwood in particular argued against categorizing works such as *[the Handmaid's Tale](/wiki/The_Handmaid%27s_Tale "The Handmaid's Tale")* as science fiction; instead she labeled this novel, *Oryx and Crake*, and *[The Testaments](/wiki/The_Testaments "The Testaments")* as [speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction"),[[242]](#cite_note-Wilderness-242) and she criticized science fiction as "talking squids in outer space."[[243]](#cite_note-langford-243)

In his book *[The Western Canon](/wiki/The_Western_Canon "The Western Canon")*, literary critic [Harold Bloom](/wiki/Harold_Bloom "Harold Bloom") includes the novels *[Brave New World](/wiki/Brave_New_World "Brave New World")*, [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem")'s *[Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(novel) "Solaris (novel)")*, [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut")'s *[Cat's Cradle](/wiki/Cat%27s_Cradle "Cat's Cradle")*, and *[The Left Hand of Darkness](/wiki/The_Left_Hand_of_Darkness "The Left Hand of Darkness")* as culturally and aesthetically significant works of Western literature, though Lem actively spurned the label *science fiction*.[[244]](#cite_note-SFWA-244)

In her 1976 essay "Science Fiction and Mrs Brown", [Ursula K. Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin") was asked, "Can a science fiction writer write a novel?" She answered that "I believe that all novels ... deal with [character](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)")... The great novelists have brought us to see whatever they wish us to see through some character. Otherwise, they would not be novelists, but poets, historians, or pamphleteers."[[245]](#cite_note-harpercollins-245)

[Orson Scott Card](/wiki/Orson_Scott_Card "Orson Scott Card") is best known for his 1985 science fiction novel *[Ender's Game](/wiki/Ender%27s_Game "Ender's Game")*; he has postulated that in science fiction, the message and intellectual significance of the work are contained within the story itself—therefore the genre can omit accepted literary devices and techniques that he characterized as [gimmicks](/wiki/Gimmick "Gimmick") or literary games.[[246]](#cite_note-google-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247)

In 1998, [Jonathan Lethem](/wiki/Jonathan_Lethem "Jonathan Lethem") wrote an [essay](/wiki/Essay "Essay") titled "Close Encounters: The Squandered Promise of Science Fiction" in the *[Village Voice](/wiki/Village_Voice "Village Voice")*. In this essay, he recalled the time in 1973 when [Thomas Pynchon](/wiki/Thomas_Pynchon "Thomas Pynchon")'s novel *[Gravity's Rainbow](/wiki/Gravity%27s_Rainbow "Gravity's Rainbow")* was nominated for the [Nebula Award](/wiki/Nebula_Award "Nebula Award") and was passed over in favor of Arthur C. Clarke's novel *[Rendezvous with Rama](/wiki/Rendezvous_with_Rama "Rendezvous with Rama")*; Lethem suggests that this point stands as "a hidden tombstone marking the death of the hope that SF was about to merge with the mainstream."[[248]](#cite_note-encounters-248) In the same year, science fiction author and physicist [Gregory Benford](/wiki/Gregory_Benford "Gregory Benford") wrote that "SF is perhaps the defining genre of the twentieth century, although its conquering armies are still camped outside the [Rome](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") of the literary citadels."[[249]](#cite_note-september-249)

## Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Community")]

### Authors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Authors")]

See also: [List of science fiction authors](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_authors "List of science fiction authors")

Science fiction has been written by authors from diverse [cultural](/wiki/Cultural_diversity "Cultural diversity") and geographical backgrounds. Among submissions to the science fiction publisher [Tor Books](/wiki/Tor_Books "Tor Books"), men account for 78% and women account for 22% (according to 2013 statistics from the publisher).[[250]](#cite_note-250) A [controversy](/wiki/2015_Hugo_Awards_controversy "2015 Hugo Awards controversy") about voting slates for the 2015 [Hugo Awards](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award") highlighted a tension in the science fiction community between two things: a trend toward increasingly diverse works and authors being honored by awards, and a reaction by groups of authors and fans who preferred more "traditional" science fiction.[[251]](#cite_note-The_A.V._Club_6_April_2015-251)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Awards")]

Main article: [List of science fiction awards](/wiki/List_of_science_fiction_awards "List of science fiction awards")

Among the most significant and well-known awards for science fiction are the [Hugo Award](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award") for [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature"), presented by the [World Science Fiction Society](/wiki/World_Science_Fiction_Society "World Science Fiction Society") at [Worldcon](/wiki/Worldcon "Worldcon"), and voted on by fans;[[252]](#cite_note-252) the [Nebula Award](/wiki/Nebula_Award "Nebula Award") for literature, presented by the [Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America](/wiki/Science_Fiction_and_Fantasy_Writers_of_America "Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America"), and voted on by the community of authors;[[253]](#cite_note-253) the [John W. Campbell Memorial Award for Best Science Fiction Novel](/wiki/John_W._Campbell_Memorial_Award_for_Best_Science_Fiction_Novel "John W. Campbell Memorial Award for Best Science Fiction Novel"), presented by a jury of writers;[[254]](#cite_note-254) and the [Theodore Sturgeon Memorial Award](/wiki/Theodore_Sturgeon_Award "Theodore Sturgeon Award") for [short fiction](/wiki/Short_story "Short story"), presented by a jury.[[255]](#cite_note-255) One notable award for science fiction films and TV programs is the [Saturn Award](/wiki/Saturn_Award "Saturn Award"), which is presented annually by [The Academy of Science Fiction, Fantasy, and Horror Films](/wiki/Academy_of_Science_Fiction,_Fantasy_and_Horror_Films "Academy of Science Fiction, Fantasy and Horror Films").[[256]](#cite_note-256)

There are other national awards, like Canada's [Prix Aurora Awards](/wiki/Prix_Aurora_Awards "Prix Aurora Awards"),[[257]](#cite_note-257) regional awards, like the [Endeavour Award](/wiki/Endeavour_Award "Endeavour Award") presented at [Orycon](/wiki/OryCon "OryCon") for works from the [U.S. Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest"),[[258]](#cite_note-258) and special interest or [subgenre](/wiki/Subgenre "Subgenre") awards such as the [Chesley Award](/wiki/Chesley_Award "Chesley Award") for art, presented by the Association of Science Fiction & Fantasy Artists,[[259]](#cite_note-259) or the [World Fantasy Award](/wiki/World_Fantasy_Award "World Fantasy Award") for fantasy.[[260]](#cite_note-260) Magazines may organize reader polls, notably the [Locus Award](/wiki/Locus_Award "Locus Award").[[261]](#cite_note-261)

### Conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Conventions")]

Main article: [Science fiction convention](/wiki/Science_fiction_convention "Science fiction convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Sfcon-reading-ddb.jpg/250px-Sfcon-reading-ddb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sfcon-reading-ddb.jpg)

Writer [Pamela Dean](/wiki/Pamela_Dean "Pamela Dean") reading at the Minneapolis convention known as [Minicon](/wiki/Minicon "Minicon") in 2006

[Conventions](/wiki/Convention_(meeting) "Convention (meeting)") (often abbreviated by fans as *cons*, such as [Comic-con](/wiki/Comic-con "Comic-con")) are held in cities around the world; these cater to a local, regional, national, or international membership.[[262]](#cite_note-262)[[50]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica-50)[[263]](#cite_note-263) General-interest conventions cover all aspects of science fiction, while others focus on a particular interest such as [media fandom](/wiki/Media_fandom "Media fandom") or [filk music](/wiki/Filking "Filking").[[264]](#cite_note-264)[[265]](#cite_note-265) Most science fiction conventions are organized by volunteers in non-profit groups, though most media-oriented events are organized by commercial promoters.[[266]](#cite_note-266)

### Fandom and fanzines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Science_fiction&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Fandom and fanzines")]

Main articles: [Science fiction fandom](/wiki/Science_fiction_fandom "Science fiction fandom") and [Science-fiction fanzine](/wiki/Science-fiction_fanzine "Science-fiction fanzine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/MGNTNJNRNL1938.jpg/250px-MGNTNJNRNL1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:MGNTNJNRNL1938.jpg)

Cover of *Imagination*, a fanzine published by Forrest J. Ackerman, January 1938, drawn by Jim Mooney

Science fiction [fandom](/wiki/Science_fiction_fandom "Science fiction fandom") emerged from the letters column in *[Amazing Stories](/wiki/Amazing_Stories "Amazing Stories")* magazine. Fans began writing letters to each other, and then assembling their comments in informal publications that became known as [*fanzines*](/wiki/Fanzine "Fanzine").[[267]](#cite_note-fanzine_history-267) Once in regular communication, these fans wanted to meet in person, so they organized local clubs.[[267]](#cite_note-fanzine_history-267)[[268]](#cite_note-fancyclopedia_con-268) During the 1930s, the first science fiction [conventions](/wiki/Science_fiction_conventions "Science fiction conventions") gathered fans from a larger area.[[268]](#cite_note-fancyclopedia_con-268)

The earliest organized online fandom was the SF Lovers Community, originally a [mailing list](/wiki/Mailing_list "Mailing list") in the late 1970s, with a text [archive file](/wiki/File_archiver "File archiver") that was updated regularly.[[269]](#cite_note-sf-lovers_hist-269) In the 1980s, [Usenet](/wiki/Usenet "Usenet") groups greatly expanded the circle of fans [online](/wiki/Online_and_offline "Online and offline").[[270]](#cite_note-270) In the 1990s, the development of the [World-Wide Web](/wiki/World-Wide_Web "World-Wide Web") increased online fandom through [websites](/wiki/Website "Website") devoted to science fiction and related [genres](/wiki/Genre "Genre") in all media.[[271]](#cite_note-fan_clubhouse-271)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
